mainmatter.p: A dcpo is called pointed when it has a bottom element \bot under the information order.

frontmatter.title: Suprema of semidirected subsets in pointed dcpos

mainmatter.p: A dcpo A is pointed if and only if it is closed under suprema of semidirected subsets.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Suppose that A is closed under suprema of semidirected subsets. In particular, we may consider the supremum \bigsqcup { \mathopen {} \left \{ \right \} \mathclose {}} of the empty set. This is evidently the bottom element, using the universal property of the supremum.

Conversely, suppose that A is pointed and let I \subseteq A be semidirected. We may replace I \subseteq A by the directed subset I' = I \cup { \mathopen {} \left \{ \bot \right \} \mathclose {}}; the inclusion I \subseteq I' is clearly cofinal, so the supremum of I' is also the supremum of I.

mainmatter.p: A pointed dcpo carries a (unique) \mathbb {L}-algebra structure.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: The algebra structure is unique if it exists because \mathbb {L} is a Kock–Zöberlein monad, i.e. a monad for which the structure maps of algebras are left adjoint to the unit in the order-enrichment. Therefore, it suffices to show that there merely exists an algebra structure { \mathsf { L } {A} } \xrightarrow {{ \alpha }}{ A } for any pointed dcpo A.

We define the structure map \alpha to take u \in \mathsf { L } {A} to the supremum of the semidirected subset { \mathopen {} \left \{ x \in A \mid u = \eta _Ax \right \} \mathclose {}}, so we may write \alpha u = \bigsqcup _{p:u{ \downarrow }}u[p]. It is not difficult to see that this assignment is continuous.
The unit law for the algebra is trivial. The multiplication law is verified as follows:

mainmatter.p: Let { \mathopen {} \left ( A, \alpha \right ) \mathclose {}} be a \mathbb {L}-algebra; then the underlying dcpo A is pointed.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: To define the bottom element of A, we choose \alpha { \mathopen {} \left ( \bot , \lambda \_ . \star \right ) \mathclose {}}. Fixing n \in A, we must check that \alpha { \mathopen {} \left ( \bot , \lambda \_ . \star \right ) \mathclose {}} \sqsubseteq n. Because \mathsf { L } is a Kock–Zöberlein monad and thus we have \alpha \dashv \eta _{A}, it is equivalent to observe that { \mathopen {} \left ( \bot , \lambda \_ . \star \right ) \mathclose {}} \sqsubseteq \eta _{A}n.

mainmatter.p: Let { \mathopen {} \left ( A, \alpha \right ) \mathclose {}} and { \mathopen {} \left ( B, \beta \right ) \mathclose {}} be two \mathbb {L}-algebras; then a morphism of dcpos { A } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ B } is a homomorphism of algebras if and only if it preserves the bottom element.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: It is clear that a homomorphism of algebras must preserve the bottom element. On the other hand, suppose that { A } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ B } preserves the bottom element to check that the following diagram commutes:
Because both composites preserve the bottom element, it is enough to observe that they both take u \in TA to the supremum of the following semidirected subset:

mainmatter.p: The category of \mathbb {L}-algebras and homomorphisms is (canonically) equivalent to the category of pointed dcpos and bottom-preserving morphisms of dcpos. It follows that pointed dcpos are monadic over dcpos.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Having a bottom element and being an algebra are both properties of dcpos, because these structures are uniquely determined. Therefore, we will argue that pointed dcpos with bottom-preserving morphisms form the same subcategory of \mathsf { dcpo } as the category of algebras with algebra-preserving maps. We have already seen that pointed structure and algebra structure are interchangeable:
To finish, we recall that a morphism of dcpos preserves algebra structure if and only if it preserves the bottom element.

mainmatter.p: By virtue of the monadicity result, we will refer to pointed dcpos and \mathbb {L}-algebras interchangeably.

We will deduce the closure of \mathsf { dcpo } ^{ \mathbb {L} } under connected colimits using general results about monads. Then we use the results of Linton to deduce the cocompleteness of \mathsf { dcpo } ^{ \mathbb {L} }. Note that the proof establishing that the lift monad preserves connected colimits has an error in it. I have a fix for it, suggested by Ohad Kammar, but I haven't written it down yet here.
For any dcpo A, the following is a co-comma square in the posetal 2-category of dcpos, where \mathsf { L } takes a dcpo to its lift or Scott-open partial map classifier.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We fix another lax square of the following form:

We must show that there exists a unique morphism \mathsf { L } {A} \xrightarrow {d}D factoring the square above through { \mathopen {} \left \{ d_ \bot \sqsubseteq d_A \right \} \mathclose {}} as follows:

We will define \mathsf { L } {A} \xrightarrow {d}D to take a ^{ ? } : \mathsf { L } {A} to the least upper bound of the directed subset U = { \mathopen {} \left \{ d_ \bot \right \} \mathclose {}} \cup { \mathopen {} \left \{ d_A a \mid a ^{ ? } = \eta _Aa \right \} \mathclose {}} \subseteq D. As this assignment evidently has the correct factorization property, it remains just to check that it is continuous. Letting V be a directed subset of \mathsf { L } {A}, we compute:
For uniqueness, suppose that we have two morphisms \mathsf { L } {A} \xrightarrow {d,d'}D factoring our square in the sense described above and fix a ^{ ? } : \mathsf { L } {A}:

mainmatter.p: Let A_ \bullet be a diagram of dcpos indexed in a connected category \mathcal {I}. Then the lifting endofunctor \mathsf { L } on dcpos preserves the colimit of A_ \bullet.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Let A_ \bullet \xrightarrow { \iota _ \bullet } { \mathopen {} \left \{ A_ \infty \right \} \mathclose {}} be a universal cocone; we must show that \mathsf { L } {A_ \bullet } \xrightarrow { \mathsf { L } { \iota _ \bullet }} { \mathopen {} \left \{ \mathsf { L } {A_ \infty } \right \} \mathclose {}} is universal as well, i.e. show any cocone \mathsf { L } {A_ \bullet } \xrightarrow {d_ \bullet } { \mathopen {} \left \{ D \right \} \mathclose {}} factors uniquely through \mathsf { L } {A_ \bullet } \xrightarrow { \mathsf { L } { \iota _ \bullet }} { \mathopen {} \left \{ \mathsf { L } {A_ \infty } \right \} \mathclose {}} in the following sense:

Using the universal property of \mathsf { L } {A_ \infty } as a co-comma dcpo, we may define \mathsf { L } {A_ \infty } \xrightarrow {d_ \infty }D to be the universal map determined by a certain lax square in the following configuration:

First we define { \mathbf {1} _{ } } \xrightarrow {{ h_ \bot }}{ D } to be the element d_i \bot determined by an arbitrary object i \in \mathcal {I}. To see that this is well-defined, fix i,j \in \mathcal {I} to check that d_i \bot =d_j \bot. As \mathcal {I} is connected, we may proceed by induction on a zigzag i \leadsto j \in \mathcal {I}; ultimately, this amounts to checking that for any span i \leftarrow k \rightarrow j in \mathcal {I}, we have d_i \bot =d_k \bot =d_j \bot. This follows from the strictness of lifted morphisms as well as the naturality of the cocone d_ \bullet.
Next we define { A_ \infty } \xrightarrow {{ h _{ A_ \infty } }}{ D } using the universal property of the colimiting cocone { A_ \bullet } \xrightarrow {{ \iota _ \bullet }}{ A_ \infty }:

To arrange h _{ A_ \infty } and h_ \bot into the desired lax square, we must check that for all a_ \infty :A_ \infty we have h_ \bot \sqsubseteq h _{ A_ \infty } a_ \infty. Fixing arbitrary i \in \mathcal {I} and a \in A_i, it suffices to check that h_ \bot \sqsubseteq h _{ A_ \infty } \iota _{ i } a. As h_ \bot =d_i \bot and h _{ A_ \infty } \iota _ia = d_i \eta _{ A_i } a, our goal follows directly from the monotonicity of { \mathsf { L } {A_i} } \xrightarrow {{ d_i }}{ D }.

p.mark: The reasoning above is not correct, we do not know that we can restrict attention to a \in A_i. This is indeed the case, but need to make it explicit.

block.p: Thus we have the desired lax square and a unique morphism { \mathsf { L } {A_ \infty } } \xrightarrow {{ h }}{ D } factoring it through the co-comma square that defines \mathsf { L } {A_ \infty }:

Our goal is to show that we may choose the universal map { \mathsf { L } {A_ \infty } } \xrightarrow {{ d_ \infty }}{ D } corresponding to the cocone { \mathsf { L } {A_ \bullet } } \xrightarrow {{ d_ \bullet }}{ { \mathopen {} \left \{ D \right \} \mathclose {}} } to be the morphism h defined above. First we must prove that the following triangle commutes:
Using the universal property of the co-comma squares for each \mathsf { L } {A_i}, it suffices to check that two diagrams commute:

li.p: We must check that the outer triangle below commutes:
Rewriting using the strictness of lifted morphisms, this amounts to the established fact that h \bot = h_ \bot.
We must check that the outer triangle below commutes:
Rewriting with established equations, it suffices to observe that the outer diagram below commutes:

ol.p: Finally, we must check that any two morphisms { \mathsf { L } {A_ \infty } } \xrightarrow {{ k,k' }}{ D } factoring { \mathsf { L } {A_ \bullet } } \xrightarrow {{ d_ \bullet }}{ { \mathopen {} \left \{ D \right \} \mathclose {}} } through { \mathsf { L } {A_ \bullet } } \xrightarrow {{ \mathsf { L } { \iota _ \bullet } }}{ { \mathopen {} \left \{ \mathsf { L } {A_ \infty } \right \} \mathclose {}} } are equal. To that end, we will use the universal property of the co-comma square defining \mathsf { L } {A_ \infty } once more to reduce this to checking that k,k' agree on \bot and on \eta _{ A_ \bullet }. The former follows directly from the strictness of \mathsf { L } { \iota _ \bullet }, as we have k \bot = k { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf { L } \iota _ \bullet \bot \right ) \mathclose {}} =d_ \bullet \bot. For the latter, we must check that k \circ \eta _{ A_ \infty } =k' \circ \eta _{ A_ \infty }; by the universal property of the colimiting cocone { A_ \bullet } \xrightarrow {{ \iota _ \bullet }}{ A_ \infty }, it suffices to check that k \circ \eta _{ A_ \infty } \circ \iota _ \bullet = k' \circ \eta _{ A_ \infty } \circ \iota _ \bullet. By naturality of the unit, these are both equal to d_ \bullet \circ \eta _{ A_ \bullet }.

mainmatter.p: Let \mathbb {L} = { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf { L } , \eta , \mu \right ) \mathclose {}} be the lifting monad on dcpos; the Eilenberg–Moore category \mathsf { dcpo } ^{ \mathbb {L} } is closed under connected colimits, and these colimits are created by the forgetful functor { \mathsf { dcpo } ^{ \mathbb {L} } } \xrightarrow {{ \mathsf { U } ^{ \mathbb {L} } }}{ \mathsf { dcpo } }.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: This follows from the following facts:

ol.li: The category of dcpos is (even constructively) known to be cocomplete.
The forgetful functor { \mathsf { dcpo } ^{ \mathbb {L} } } \xrightarrow {{ \mathsf { U } ^{ \mathbb {L} } }}{ \mathsf { dcpo } } creates whatever colimits \mathsf { L } preserves.

mainmatter.p: The Eilenberg–Moore category \mathsf { dcpo } ^{ \mathbb {L} } for the lifting monad \mathbb {L} = { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf { L } , \eta , \mu \right ) \mathclose {}} on dcpos has all small coproducts.
This is the first part of Corollary 2 of Linton's Coequalizers in categories of algebras.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: The coproduct of lift-algebras is computed as a reflexive coequalizer, whose existence we have already established. Given an index set I and a diagram { I } \xrightarrow {{ X _{ \bullet } }}{ \mathsf { dcpo } ^{ \mathbb {L} } }, the colimit of the following reflexive pair is the coproduct of { I } \xrightarrow {{ X _{ \bullet } }}{ \mathsf { dcpo } ^{ \mathbb {L} } } as shown by Linton:

frontmatter.title: The category of lift-algebras is cocomplete

mainmatter.p: The Eilenberg–Moore category \mathsf { dcpo } ^{ \mathbb {L} } for the lifting monad \mathbb {L} = { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf { L } , \eta , \mu \right ) \mathclose {}} on dcpos is cocomplete.
mainmatter.block: Proof. This follows because \mathsf { dcpo } ^{ \mathbb {L} } under connected colimits and coproducts

mainmatter.p: I am accumulating an understanding of constructive domain theory — that is, the domain theory that can be carried out in an elementary topos with a natural numbers object. These notes are based on the work of Tom de Jong and Martín Escardó; these notes, however, assume the propositional resizing principle whereas de Jong and Escardó explore a predicative version domain theory based on large and locally small domains.

In this section, we gather some useful definitions and results concerning creation of (co)limits. The reason for stating these things explicitly is that the usual definitions of created (co)limits are deeply irrational in a variety of incompatible ways, so it is important to fix definitions that can work from the start. It would be good to reformulate this in terms of displayed categories of algebras.

Let { \mathcal {C} } \xrightarrow {{ U }}{ \mathcal {D} } be a functor and let \mathcal {I} be a category. The functor U is said to create (co)limits of \mathcal {I}-figures when for any diagram { \mathcal {I} } \xrightarrow {{ C_ \bullet }}{ \mathcal {C} } such that \mathcal {I} \xrightarrow {C_ \bullet } \mathcal {C} \xrightarrow {U} \mathcal {D} has a (co)limit, then C_ \bullet has a (co)limit that is both preserved and reflected by U.
We aim to understand sufficient conditions for a forgetful functor to create colimits. As we will show below, it is sufficient for these colimits to be preserved the the monad.

frontmatter.title: Algebra structure on the colimit apex

mainmatter.p: Let \mathcal {C} be a category and let \mathbb {T} = { \mathopen {} \left ( T, \eta , \mu \right ) \mathclose {}} be a monad on \mathcal {C}, writing { \mathcal {C} ^{ \mathbb {T} } } \xrightarrow {{ U }}{ \mathcal {C} } for the forgetful functor from the Eilenberg–Moore category \mathcal {C} ^{ \mathbb {T} }. Suppose that T preserves colimits of \mathcal {I}-figures for a given small category \mathcal {I}. Let { \mathcal {I} } \xrightarrow {{ X_ \bullet }}{ \mathcal {C} ^{ \mathbb {T} } } be a diagram in \mathcal {C} ^{ \mathbb {T} } such that \mathcal {I} \xrightarrow {X_ \bullet } \mathcal {C} ^{ \mathbb {T} } \xrightarrow {U} \mathcal {C} has a universal cocone { UX_ \bullet } \xrightarrow {{ c_ \bullet }}{ { \mathopen {} \left \{ C \right \} \mathclose {}} }. We may lift C to an essentially unique \mathbb {T}-algebra structure \bar {C} with U \bar {C}=C in a canonical way such that { UX_ \bullet } \xrightarrow {{ c_ \bullet }}{ { \mathopen {} \left \{ C \right \} \mathclose {}} } lifts to a cocone of algebras { X _{ \bullet } } \xrightarrow {{ \bar {c} _{ \bullet } }}{ \bar {C} } with U \bar {c}_ \bullet =c_ \bullet.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: By assumption, { TUX_ \bullet } \xrightarrow {{ Tc_ \bullet }}{ { \mathopen {} \left \{ TC \right \} \mathclose {}} } is a universal cocone. We may define a further cocone TUX_ \bullet \xrightarrow { \alpha _{X_ \bullet }}UX_ \bullet \xrightarrow {c_ \bullet } { \mathopen {} \left \{ C \right \} \mathclose {}}. The universal property of Tc_ \bullet then gives us a unique morphism { TC } \xrightarrow {{ \beta }}{ C } satisfying the following condition:
We must show that the map { TC } \xrightarrow {{ \beta }}{ C } depicted above satisfies the axioms of a \mathbb {T}-algebra.

li.p: To show that C \xrightarrow { \eta _C}TC \xrightarrow { \beta }C is the identity, we note that { UX_ \bullet } \xrightarrow {{ c_ \bullet }}{ { \mathopen {} \left \{ C \right \} \mathclose {}} } is universal so it suffices to check that UX_ \bullet \xrightarrow {c_ \bullet } { \mathopen {} \left \{ C \right \} \mathclose {}} \xrightarrow { { \mathopen {} \left \{ \eta _C \right \} \mathclose {}} } { \mathopen {} \left \{ TC \right \} \mathclose {}} \xrightarrow { { \mathopen {} \left \{ \beta \right \} \mathclose {}} }{ { \mathopen {} \left \{ C \right \} \mathclose {}} } is equal to c_ \bullet by the uniqueness property. This follows by inspection of the following commuting diagram, in which the top square is the naturality of the unit:
We must check that the the following multiplication square commutes:

By assumption, we know that { TTUX_ \bullet } \xrightarrow {{ TTc_ \bullet }}{ { \mathopen {} \left \{ TTC \right \} \mathclose {}} } is a universal cocone; using this fact as well as the naturality of \mu, it suffices to check that the following diagram commutes:
We compute algebraically, using the algebra property of X among other assumptions:

block.p: Hence we may define a \mathbb {T}-algebra structure \bar {C} with U \bar {C}=C, setting { TC } \xrightarrow {{ \alpha _{ \bar {C}} }}{ C } to be \beta. That c_ \bullet lifts to a cocone of algebras is exactly the defining condition of \alpha _{ \bar {C}}= \beta via the universal property of { TUX_ \bullet } \xrightarrow {{ Tc_ \bullet }}{ { \mathopen {} \left \{ TC \right \} \mathclose {}} }; uniqueness of the algebra structure with this property follows from the same universal property.

frontmatter.title: Reflection of colimits by the forgetful functor

mainmatter.p: Let \mathcal {C} be a category and let \mathbb {T} = { \mathopen {} \left ( T, \eta , \mu \right ) \mathclose {}} be a monad on \mathcal {C}, writing { \mathcal {C} ^{ \mathbb {T} } } \xrightarrow {{ U }}{ \mathcal {C} } for the forgetful functor from the Eilenberg–Moore category \mathcal {C} ^{ \mathbb {T} }. If T preserves colimits of \mathcal {I}-figures for a given indexing category \mathcal {I}, then { \mathcal {C} ^{ \mathbb {T} } } \xrightarrow {{ U }}{ \mathcal {C} } reflects colimits of \mathcal {I}-figures.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Let { \mathcal {I} } \xrightarrow {{ X_ \bullet }}{ \mathcal {C} ^{ \mathbb {T} } } be a diagram in \mathcal {C} ^{ \mathbb {T} } and let { X_ \bullet } \xrightarrow {{ y_ \bullet }}{ { \mathopen {} \left \{ Y \right \} \mathclose {}} } be a cocone in \mathcal {C} ^{ \mathbb {T} } such that { UX_ \bullet } \xrightarrow {{ Uy_ \bullet }}{ { \mathopen {} \left \{ UY \right \} \mathclose {}} } is a universal cocone for UX_ \bullet = \mathcal {I} \xrightarrow {X_ \bullet } \mathcal {C} ^{ \mathbb {T} } \xrightarrow {U} \mathcal {C}. We must show that { X_ \bullet } \xrightarrow {{ y_ \bullet }}{ { \mathopen {} \left \{ Y \right \} \mathclose {}} } is a universal cocone.

In particular, we must factor any cocone { X_ \bullet } \xrightarrow {{ z_ \bullet }}{ { \mathopen {} \left \{ Z \right \} \mathclose {}} } uniquely through { X_ \bullet } \xrightarrow {{ y_ \bullet }}{ { \mathopen {} \left \{ Y \right \} \mathclose {}} }. Because Uy_ \bullet is universal, there exists a unique { Y } \xrightarrow {{ h }}{ Z } such that the following diagram commutes:
We must check that h is a homomorphism of \mathbb {T}-algebras:
To see that \alpha _Y;h = Th; \alpha _Z it suffices to observe that they both factor the cocone

through the cocone { TUX_ \bullet } \xrightarrow {{ TUy_ \bullet }}{ { \mathopen {} \left \{ TUY \right \} \mathclose {}} }, which is universal by our assumption that T preserves \mathcal {I}-colimits.

li.p: First we must check that the following diagram commutes:
This holds immediately from the homomorphism property of y_ \bullet.
Second, we must check that the following diagram commutes:
This follows from the factorization Uy_ \bullet ;h=Uz_ \bullet and the homomorphism property of z_ \bullet.

frontmatter.title: Creation of colimits by the forgetful functor

mainmatter.p: Let \mathcal {C} be a category and let \mathbb {T} = { \mathopen {} \left ( T, \eta , \mu \right ) \mathclose {}} be a monad on \mathcal {C}, writing { \mathcal {C} ^{ \mathbb {T} } } \xrightarrow {{ U }}{ \mathcal {C} } for the forgetful functor from the Eilenberg–Moore category \mathcal {C} ^{ \mathbb {T} }. If T preserves colimits of \mathcal {I}-figures for a given small category \mathcal {I}, then { \mathcal {C} ^{ \mathbb {T} } } \xrightarrow {{ U }}{ \mathcal {C} } creates colimits of \mathcal {I}-figures.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Let { \mathcal {I} } \xrightarrow {{ X_ \bullet }}{ \mathcal {C} ^{ \mathbb {T} } } be a diagram such that \mathcal {I} \xrightarrow {X_ \bullet } \mathcal {C} ^{ \mathbb {T} } \xrightarrow {U} \mathcal {C} has a (co)limit cocone { UX_ \bullet } \xrightarrow {{ c_ \bullet }}{ { \mathopen {} \left \{ C \right \} \mathclose {}} }. Let \bar {C}= { \mathopen {} \left ( C, \alpha _{ \bar {C}} \right ) \mathclose {}} be the canonical algebra structure on the colimit apex C such that { UX_ \bullet } \xrightarrow {{ c_ \bullet }}{ { \mathopen {} \left \{ C \right \} \mathclose {}} } lifts to a cocone of algebras. Then we conclude that this cocone is universal because U reflects colimits of \mathcal {I}-figures. It is evident (by definition) that U preserves this colimit.

mainmatter.p: These notes were prepared by Jon Sterling, based in part on discussions with Martín Hötzel Escardó and Tom de Jong. Any errors belong to Jon Sterling.

frontmatter.title: The positive elements of a pointed dcpo

mainmatter.p: An element u of a pointed dpco A is called positive when for any semidirected subset I \subseteq A, if u \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup {I} then I is inhabited. We will write \mathsf { pos } \, {u} to mean that u is a positive element.
(This is adapted from Johnstone’s definition of positive elements of frames to the case of dcpos by Tom de Jong.)

frontmatter.title: The bottom element is not positive

mainmatter.p: The bottom element \bot of a pointed dcpo A is not positive.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Suppose that \bot was positive. The empty set { \mathopen {} \left \{ \right \} \mathclose {}} \subseteq A is semidirected and \bot \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup { \mathopen {} \left \{ \right \} \mathclose {}} = \bot, but nonetheless { \mathopen {} \left \{ \right \} \mathclose {}} is not inhabited.

mainmatter.p: Let i \sqsubseteq j in a pointed dcpo A such that i is positive; then j is positive.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Fixing semidirected J \subseteq A such that j \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup {J}, we must show that J is inhabited. This follows from our assumption that i is positive becuase i \sqsubseteq j \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup {J}.

frontmatter.title: Closure of positivity under directed suprema

mainmatter.p: Let A be a pointed dcpo; the positivity predicate { \Gamma A } \xrightarrow {{ \mathsf { pos } }}{ \Omega } is closed under directed suprema.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Let J \subseteq A be a directed subset such that every u \in J is positive; we must show that \bigsqcup {J} is positive. Fixing a subdirected subset I \subseteq A such that \bigsqcup {J} \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup I, we must check that I is inhabited. The inequality \bigsqcup {J} \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup I holds if and only if u \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup {I} for all u \in u; as J is directed, we therefore have some positive u \in A such that u \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup {I} from which it follows that I is inhabited.

frontmatter.title: The subdcpo spanned by positive elements

mainmatter.p: Let A be a pointed dcpo; then the subposet A^+ \subseteq A spanned by positive is a dcpo.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: This follows from the closure of positive elements in an algebra under directed suprema.

frontmatter.title: The positive part of a semidirected subset is semidirected

mainmatter.p: Let I \subseteq A be a semidirected subset of a pointed dcpo A; then the intersection I \cap A^+ is semidirected, where A^+ is the subdcpo spanned by positive elements.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Let i,j be two positive elements of I; we must show that there exists a positive element k \in I such that i \sqsubseteq k and j \sqsubseteq k. This follows immediately from the directedness of I and the fact that positivity is monotone.

frontmatter.title: Positivity = definedness in a lifted dcpo

mainmatter.p: The positive elements of a lifted dcpo \mathsf { L } {A} are exactly the defined elements, i.e. those lying in the image of { A } \xrightarrow {{ \eta }}{ \mathsf { L } {A} }.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Let a \in A be an element of the dcpo A. To show that \eta _A a is positive, we fix a semidirected subset U \subseteq \mathsf { L } {A} such that \eta _A a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup U to show that U is inhabited. The inequality implies that \top \sqsubseteq \exists u \in U. u{ \downarrow }, so we know that U is inhabited.

Conversely, suppose that u \in \mathsf { L } {A} is a positive element. Let U be the semidirected family { \mathopen {} \left \{ v \mid v = u \land u \downarrow \right \} \mathclose {}}; we evidently have u \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup {U}, so U is inhabited. Thus it follows that u is defined.

frontmatter.title: Characterization of positivity in terms of partial elements

mainmatter.p: Let A be a pointed dcpo. Then an element a:A is positive if and only if for any partial element u: LA such that a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p:u{ \downarrow }}u[p], we have u{ \downarrow }= \top.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Therefore, we first suppose that any u: LA is defined supposing a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p:u{ \downarrow }}u[p] to check that a is positive. Fixing a semidirected subset I \subseteq A such that a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup {I}, we must show that I is inhabited. We consider the partial element u= { \mathopen {} \left ( \exists i \in I. \top , \lambda \_ . \bigsqcup {I} \right ) \mathclose {}} :LA, and observe that \bigsqcup {I}= \bigsqcup _{i \in I} \bigsqcup {I}= \bigsqcup _{p:u{ \downarrow }}u[p], and so we have a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p:u{ \downarrow }}u[p] and thus it follows that u{ \downarrow }= \top and so I is inhabited.

Conversely, if a is positive, it follows that any partial element u is defined when a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p:u{ \downarrow }}u[p], because the subset { \mathopen {} \left \{ y \mid y=a \land u{ \downarrow } \right \} \mathclose {}} is semidirected.

mainmatter.p: It follows from our characterization of positivity in terms of partial elements that an element a: A of a pointed dcpo A is positive if and only if it is strictly above the bottom element, i.e. \bot \sqsubset a.
(See also Proposition 6.2.12 of Tom de Jong’s thesis.)

So far we have studied positivity as an order-theoretic property of domains; in this section, we explore a non-order-theoretic generalization of this condition that is applicable in the setting of axiomatic domain theory. This notion is inspired by the alternative characterization positivity in terms of partial elements.

frontmatter.title: The Sierpiński interval of a dominion

mainmatter.p: Let \mathcal {C} be a category with pullbacks, a terminal object and let \mathcal {M} be a dominion on \mathcal {C}. A Sierpiński space for \mathcal {M} is defined to be a universal \mathcal {M}-partial map, i.e. a morphism { \mathbf {1} _{ \mathcal {C} } } \xrightarrow {{ \top }}{ \Sigma } \in \mathcal {M} such that for any { U } \xrightarrow {{ i }}{ A } \in \mathcal {M} there exists a unique map { A } \xrightarrow {{ \chi _i }}{ \Sigma } making the following square cartesian:

When \mathcal {C} has an initial object \mathbf {0} _{ \mathcal {C} } and every { \mathbf {0} _{ \mathcal {C} } } \xrightarrow {{ }}{ A } lies in \mathcal {M}, the Sierpiński space becomes an “interval object” { \mathopen {} \left ( \Sigma , \top , \bot \right ) \mathclose {}} where { \mathbf {1} _{ \mathcal {C} } } \xrightarrow {{ \bot }}{ \Sigma } is given as follows:
Under these circumstances, we refer to \mathcal {M} as a dominion that has a Sierpiński interval.

Let \mathcal {C} be category with pullbacks, a terminal object, and an initial object, equipped with a dominion \mathcal {M} that has a Sierpiński interval { \mathopen {} \left ( \Sigma , \top , \bot \right ) \mathclose {}}. A path from { I } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ C } to { I } \xrightarrow {{ g }}{ C } in \mathcal {C} is defined to be a morphism { \Sigma \times I } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ C } such that the following diagram commutes:
We shall write p:f \leadsto g to denote a path from f to g.

Let \mathcal {C} be category with pullbacks, a terminal object, and an initial object, equipped with a dominion \mathcal {M} that has a Sierpiński interval { \mathopen {} \left ( \Sigma , \top , \bot \right ) \mathclose {}}, such that \mathcal {M}-partial map classifiers LA exist for all A \in \mathcal {C}. We will write \mathbb {L}= { \mathopen {} \left ( L, \eta , \mu \right ) \mathclose {}} for the resulting partial map classifier monad on \mathcal {C}.

Let { \mathopen {} \left ( A, \alpha \right ) \mathclose {}} be an \mathbb {L}-algebra in \mathcal {C}, and let { C } \xrightarrow {{ x }}{ A } be a generalized element of A. We say that { C } \xrightarrow {{ x }}{ A } is positive when for any { C } \xrightarrow {{ u }}{ LA } such that there exists a path from { C } \xrightarrow {{ x }}{ A } to { C } \xrightarrow {{ u; \alpha }}{ A }, then { C } \xrightarrow {{ u }}{ LA } factors through some (necessarily unique) { C } \xrightarrow {{ y }}{ A } as below:

frontmatter.title: The two notions of positivity coincide for pointed dcpos

mainmatter.p: Let A be a pointed dcpo. Then an element x:A is a positive element of the pointed dcpo if and only if { \mathbf {1} _{ } } \xrightarrow {{ x }}{ A } is a positive element in the dominion \mathcal {O} of Scott-open immersions of dcpos.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We first observe that the latter condition is equivalent to the following:
block.blockquote: For any u \in LA, if a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: u{ \downarrow }}u[p] then u{ \downarrow }= \top.
block.p: This follows from the (constructive) characterization of the path order in dcpos. Therefore, our result follows from our earlier characterization of positive elements of a pointed dcpo in terms of partial elements.

frontmatter.title: Positive generation and open positivity predicates

mainmatter.p: In a lifted dcpo \mathsf { L } {A}, the positive elements form an open subdcpo { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf { L } {A} \right ) \mathclose {}} ^+ \cong A: thus, it follows that positivity is always an open property in classical mathematics. On the other hand, it seems that constructive mathematics does not necessarily assert that the positive elements form an open subspace in general. In this section, we grapple with this subtlety by studying the relationship between openness of A^+ and a desirable property that we shall refer to as positive generation, which states that every element of A is the supremum of its “positive part”.
We say that a pointed dcpo A has an open positivity predicate if and only if any of the following equivalent conditions hold:

ol.li: The positivity predicate { \Gamma A } \xrightarrow {{ \mathsf { pos } }}{ \Omega } tracks a continuous morphism of dcpos { A } \xrightarrow {{ \mathsf { pos } }}{ \Sigma }.
The embedding { A^+ } \xrightarrow {{ \iota ^+ }}{ A } is a Scott-open immersion.
For a directed subset I \subseteq A, if \bigsqcup {A} is positive then there exists an element a \in I that is positive in A.

mainmatter.p: Let A be a dcpo; then an element a:A is called positively generated when it is the supremum of the semidirected subset { \mathopen {} \left \{ b:A \mid b=a \land \mathsf { pos } \, {a} \right \} \mathclose {}}, i.e. we have a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf { pos } \, a}a.
An element a of a pointed dcpo A is called non-negatively generated when a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{ \lnot { \mathopen {} \left ( a= \bot \right ) \mathclose {}} }a holds.

frontmatter.title: Positively generated elements are non-negatively generated

mainmatter.p: Let a be a positively generated element of a pointed dcpo A. Then a is also non-negatively generated.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We must check that a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \lnot { \mathopen {} \left ( a = \bot \right ) \mathclose {}} }a. By assumption, it suffices to check that \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf { pos } \, a}a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \lnot { \mathopen {} \left ( a= \bot \right ) \mathclose {}} }a, which follows because the bottom element is not positive.

frontmatter.title: Bottom and non-bottom elements are non-negatively generated

mainmatter.p: In a pointed dcpo, any non-bottom element is non-negatively generated.

ol.li: Any compact element a:A for which \lnot { \mathopen {} \left ( a= \bot \right ) \mathclose {}} holds.

mainmatter.p: Hence, any element a for which it is decidable whether a= \bot is non-negatively generated.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: The bottom element is clearly non-negatively generated, as it lies beneath any element. Now fix a:A and assume \lnot { \mathopen {} \left ( a= \bot \right ) \mathclose {}}; we must check that a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \lnot { \mathopen {} \left ( a= \bot \right ) \mathclose {}} }a but the latter is equal to a.

mainmatter.p: When a pointed dcpo A satisfies either of the following equivalent conditions, we will refer to it as positively generated.

ol.li: Every element a: A is positively generated, i.e. we have a = \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf { pos } \, a}a.
For any semidirected subset I \subseteq A, we have \bigsqcup I = \bigsqcup { \mathopen {} \left \{ a \in I \mid \mathsf { pos } \, a \right \} \mathclose {}}.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: (2) implies (1) by setting I = { \mathopen {} \left \{ a \right \} \mathclose {}}. Conversely, assume that each element is the supremum of its positive part and fix a semidirected subset I \subseteq A. We compute:
block.tex: \bigsqcup { \mathopen {} \left \{ a \in I \mid \mathsf { pos } \, a \right \} \mathclose {}} = \bigsqcup _{a \in { \mathopen {} \left \{ a \in I \mid \mathsf { pos } \, a \right \} \mathclose {}} }a = \bigsqcup _{a \in I} \bigsqcup _{p \in \mathsf { pos } \, a} a = \bigsqcup _{a \in I} a

frontmatter.title: Positively generated dcpos have open positivity predicates

mainmatter.p: Let A be a positively generated pointed dcpo. Then A has an open positivity predicate.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Let I \subseteq A be a directed subset such that \bigsqcup {I} is positive; we must find a positive element of I. Because A is positively generated we know that \bigsqcup {I} \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup { \mathopen {} \left ( I \cap A^+ \right ) \mathclose {}}. Because \bigsqcup {I} is positive and I \cap A^+ is semidirected, it follows that the latter is inhabited.

mainmatter.p: Let A be a dcpo; then the lift \mathsf { L } {A} is positively generated.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Let u= { \mathopen {} \left ( \phi ,a \right ) \mathclose {}} be an element of \mathsf { L } {A}. Note that u is equal to \bigsqcup _{p: \phi }u; as definedness coincides with positivity in lifted dcpos, we have \phi = \mathsf { pos } \, u, and so we are done.

frontmatter.title: Lifted dcpos have open positivity predicates

mainmatter.p: Let A be a dcpo; then the lifting \mathsf { L } {A} has an open positivity predicate.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: It suffices to recall that a lifted dcpo is positively generated.

frontmatter.title: When is a continuous pointed dcpo positively generated?

mainmatter.p: We describe some light conditions under which a continuous dcpo is positively generated.

frontmatter.title: Non-negatively generated basis yields positive generation

mainmatter.p: Let A be a pointed continuous dcpo presented by a basis B \subseteq A such that each element of B is non-negatively generated in A. Then A is positively generated.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We must show that for any a \in A, we have a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf { pos } \, a}a. Let B_a be the subset { \mathopen {} \left \{ b \in B \mid b \ll a \right \} \mathclose {}}. By assumption, it suffices to check that \bigsqcup B_a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf { pos } \, a}a. Fixing b \in B_a, we must check that b \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf { pos } \, a}a.

As b is assumed to be non-negatively generated, we have b= \bigsqcup _{p: \lnot { \mathopen {} \left ( b= \bot \right ) \mathclose {}} }b so to check b \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf { pos } \, a}a it suffices to check that \lnot { \mathopen {} \left ( b= \bot \right ) \mathclose {}} implies that a is positive. We fix semidirected J \subseteq A such that a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup {J} = \bigsqcup { \mathopen {} \left ( { \mathopen {} \left \{ \bot \right \} \mathclose {}} \cup J \right ) \mathclose {}} to check that J is inhabited. We recall that b \ll a and a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup { \mathopen {} \left ( { \mathopen {} \left \{ \bot \right \} \mathclose {}} \cup J \right ) \mathclose {}}, so it follows by the definition of the way-below relation that either b \sqsubseteq \bot or there exists j \in J such that b \sqsubseteq j. The first case is contradictory by our assumption that \lnot { \mathopen {} \left ( b= \bot \right ) \mathclose {}}, and the second case verifies that J is inhabited, so a is positive.

frontmatter.title: Basis with decidable bottomhood yields positive generation

mainmatter.p: Let A be a pointed continuous dcpo presented by a basis B \subseteq A for each element b \in B it is decidable whether b= \bot. Then A is positively generated.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We observe that every element of B is non-negatively generated, so we may conclude that A is positively generated.

frontmatter.title: Positively generated basis yields positive generation

mainmatter.p: Let A be a pointed continuous dcpo presented by a basis B \subseteq A whose elements are all positively generated in A. Then A is positively generated.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: As positive generation implies non-negative generation for an element of a pointed dcpo, we may conclude that A is positively generated.

mainmatter.p: I am not sure how useful the following construction is. On the other hand, maybe it has a universal property at the level of posets.

frontmatter.title: The positively generated core of a dcpo

mainmatter.p: Let A be a pointed dcpo; the subposet A ^{ \mathit {pg} } \subseteq {A} spanned by positively generated elements is closed under directed suprema, and thus gives rise to a subdcpo that we shall refer to as the positively generated core of A.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Let I \subseteq A be a directed subset whose elements are all positively generated. We must check that the supremum \bigsqcup {I} is positively generated, i.e. \bigsqcup {I} \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf { pos } \, \bigsqcup {I}} \bigsqcup {I}. Fixing i \in I, we must check that i \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf { pos } \, { \bigsqcup {I}}} \bigsqcup {I}. As i is positively generated, we have i \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf { pos } \, {i}}i. As positivity is upward-closed, we have \mathsf { pos } \, {i} \sqsubseteq \mathsf { pos } \, \bigsqcup {I}. Therefore, we deduce i \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf { pos } \, i}i \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf { pos } \, { \bigsqcup {I}}} \bigsqcup {I}.

frontmatter.title: Characterizing freeness in terms of positivity

mainmatter.p: In classical mathematics, every \mathbb {L}-algebra is free; this does not appear to be asserted by constructive mathematics, but in this section we verify that freeness can be characterized in terms of positivity. The main result is that a pointed dcpo A is positively generated if and only the evident map { \mathsf { L } {A^+} } \xrightarrow {{ }}{ A } is an isomorphism.

Let A be a pointed dcpo with an open positivity predicate; we define the “purification” map { A } \xrightarrow {{ \mathsf { purify } _A }}{ \mathsf { L } {A^+} } into the lift of the subdcpo of positive elements to send a \in A to the partial element { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf { pos } \, {a}, \lambda p. a \right ) \mathclose {}}; we note:
mainmatter.tex: \mathsf { purify } _Aa = { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf { pos } \, {a}, \lambda p. a \right ) \mathclose {}} = \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf { pos } \, a} \eta _Aa
mainmatter.p: The mapping defined above is continuous because we have assumed that A has an open positivity predicate, so its domain of definition defines a Scott-open subdcpo of A.

frontmatter.title: Pointed dcpos with open positivity predicates are almost free

mainmatter.p: Let A be a pointed dcpo with an open positivity predicate; then the purification map { A } \xrightarrow {{ \mathsf { purify } _A }}{ \mathsf { L } {A^+} } is the projection part of an embedding-projection pair { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf { L } { \iota ^+}; \alpha _A \right ) \mathclose {}} \dashv \mathsf { purify } _A.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We fix { \mathopen {} \left ( \phi ,a^+ \right ) \mathclose {}} : \mathsf { L } {A^+} and compute explicitly using the fact that \mathsf { purify } _A preserves the bottom element and therefore preserves semidirected subsets:

For the deflation, we fix a:A and observe that
\alpha _A { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf { L } { \iota ^+} { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf { purify } _A a \right ) \mathclose {}} \right ) \mathclose {}} = \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf { pos } \, a} a which is evidently below a.

frontmatter.title: Positively generated pointed dcpos are free

mainmatter.p: Let A be a positively generated pointed dcpo; then A is canonically free on its subdcpo of positive elements A^+. More specifically, the embedding-projection pair { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf { L } { \iota ^+}; \alpha _A \right ) \mathclose {}} \dashv { A } \xrightarrow {{ \mathsf { purify } _A }}{ \mathsf { L } {A^+} } given by the purification map is an isomorphism of dcpos.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: First, we recall that the embedding-projection pair { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf { L } { \iota ^+}; \alpha _A \right ) \mathclose {}} \dashv \mathsf { purify } _A exists because A has an open positivity predicate by virtue of its positive generation. Therefore, the statement of this lemma is well-formed.

Because { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf { L } { \iota ^+}; \alpha _A \right ) \mathclose {}} \dashv \mathsf { purify } _A is an embedding-projection pair, it remains only to check that \mathsf { purify } _A; \mathsf { L } { \iota ^+}; \alpha _A is an inflation, i.e. for all a:A we must have a \sqsubseteq \alpha _A { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf { L } { \iota ^+} { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf { purify } _Aa \right ) \mathclose {}} \right ) \mathclose {}}. As the latter is equal to \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf { pos } \, a}a, the inflation condition follows from our assumption that A is positively generated.

mainmatter.p: A pointed dcpo A is lifted if and only if it is positively generated.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: This follows from two existing lemmas:

ol.li: Lifted dcpos (free algebras) are positively generated.
Positively generated pointed dcpos are free.

mainmatter.p: The category of \mathbb {L}-algebras and homomorphisms is (canonically) equivalent to the category of pointed dcpos and bottom-preserving morphisms of dcpos. It follows that pointed dcpos are monadic over dcpos.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Having a bottom element and being an algebra are both properties of dcpos, because these structures are uniquely determined. Therefore, we will argue that pointed dcpos with bottom-preserving morphisms form the same subcategory of \mathsf { dcpo } as the category of algebras with algebra-preserving maps. We have already seen that pointed structure and algebra structure are interchangeable:
To finish, we recall that a morphism of dcpos preserves algebra structure if and only if it preserves the bottom element.

mainmatter.p: A dcpo is called pointed when it has a bottom element \bot under the information order.

frontmatter.title: Presenting dcpos and dcpo algebras
frontmatter.meta: @article{jung-moshier-vickers-2008, author = {Achim Jung and M. Andrew Moshier and Steve Vickers}, doi = {10.1016/j.entcs.2008.10.013}, issn = {1571-0661}, journal = {Electronic Notes in Theoretical Computer Science}, keywords = {Dcpo algebras, dcpo presentations, d-topology, -topology, coverage theorems}, note = {Proceedings of the 24th Conference on the Mathematical Foundations of Programming Semantics (MFPS XXIV)}, pages = {209-229}, title = {Presenting Dcpos and Dcpo Algebras}, volume = {218}, year = {2008}, }

mainmatter.p: Dcpos can be presented by preorders of generators and inequational relations expressed as covers. Algebraic operations on the generators (possibly with their results being ideals of generators) can be extended to the dcpo presented, provided the covers are “stable” for the operations. The resulting dcpo algebra has a natural universal characterization and satisfies all the inequational laws satisfied by the generating algebra. Applications include known “coverage theorems” from locale theory.

frontmatter.title: Coequalizers in categories of algebras
frontmatter.meta: Seminar on Triples and Categorical Homology Theory

@inproceedings{linton-1969-coequalizers, address = {Berlin, Heidelberg}, author = {Linton, F. E. J.}, booktitle = {Seminar on Triples and Categorical Homology Theory}, editor = {Eckmann, B.}, isbn = {978-3-540-36091-9}, pages = {75--90}, publisher = {Springer Berlin Heidelberg}, title = {Coequalizers in categories of algebras}, year = {1969} }
